text: The surgeon begins creating a flap in the thin peritoneum to address a left-sided indirect hernia with sliding sigmoid, using a transabdominal preperitoneal (TAPP) approach.
The surgeon begins the medial dissection, focusing on separating tissue layers in the area of the left-sided indirect hernia.
The surgeon attempts to extract the indirect hernia sac from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon attempts to identify the cord structures within the surgical field, focusing on distinguishing anatomical features related to the hernia repair.
The surgeon sweeps the cord structures away from surrounding tissue, carefully separating them within the surgical field.
The surgeon visually identifies the edge of the true hernia sac, distinguishing it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers along the edge of the hernia sac, ensuring precise dissection while avoiding proximity to the bowel wall.
The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field, anticipating the identification of the vas deferens within the hernia sac area.
The surgeon continues to parietalize the cord structures, carefully separating them from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon visually inspects the surgical field for the presence of a lipoma near the hernia sac.
The surgeon completes the MPO dissection, finalizing the separation of the hernia sac from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon places a 16 by 12 cm anatomic left program mesh into the surgical field.
The surgeon proceeds to close the flap, completing the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon completes the dissection, finalizing the separation of tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon inserts an angiocath into the flap to evacuate gas, visibly reducing the distension in the surgical area.
The surgeon ensures the mesh remains flat as the surgical area is deflated, providing stability to the mesh placement.
